content_en:
Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's treatment of taharah in Tuhfat al-Muhtaj represents the definitive late-Shafi'i analysis of purification law, incorporating the cumulative scholarship of the school's development up to the tenth century AH. He engages not only with the positions of an-Nawawi and ar-Rafi'i but with the contributions of the generation of scholars between an-Nawawi and himself — particularly the 'two Shaykhs' (ash-Shaykhan) whose positions formed the authoritative core of later Shafi'i practice.

On the question of water, Ibn Hajar al-Haytami engages with the theological basis for the two-qullah threshold. He notes that the hadith of the two qullahs (Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidhi) was accepted by ash-Shafi'i as the basis for distinguishing small and large quantities, even though scholars of hadith have debated its precise grading. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami defends ash-Shafi'i's use of the hadith with the argument that even hadith of lesser strength may establish the threshold for a ruling when no stronger evidence conflicts with it and the scholarly tradition has accepted it.

For wudu, Ibn Hajar al-Haytami addresses a question of growing practical importance in his era: the use of warm or hot water for wudu. He affirms the standard Shafi'i position that any water permissible for purification may be used for wudu, including water heated to any temperature, unless the heat itself changes the water's characteristics to remove it from the tahur category (a theoretical extreme not normally encountered). He also addresses the question of using water that has cooled after being heated — the ruling does not change; the water remains fully tahur.

The treatment of touching a woman and its effect on wudu — one of the most discussed and practically significant Shafi'i positions — receives extended analysis in Tuhfat al-Muhtaj. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami presents the Shafi'i school's position (touching a marriageable woman's skin nullifies wudu), its exceptions (mahram relatives, touching over a barrier), and the competing interpretations of the Quranic phrase 'lamastum an-nisa'' (4:43). He engages with the arguments of those who interpret this as metaphorical for sexual contact (the Hanafi view) and defends the Shafi'i literal interpretation while acknowledging the scholarly weight of the alternative.

Najasah and its purification receive elaborate treatment. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami discusses the purification of contaminated land and how the growth of new earth above the contaminated layer affects the purity status for prayer. He addresses the question of extremely fine contaminants — is microscopic-level contamination legally relevant? He applies the Shafi'i principle that legal rulings follow perceptible reality, suggesting that what cannot be perceived by the normal human senses does not trigger legal rulings — a position with significant implications for the modern scientific understanding of biological contamination.

The chapter on menstruation in Tuhfat al-Muhtaj addresses the experiences of women with irregular cycles with great pastoral care. Ibn Hajar al-Haytami develops elaborate rules for the mustahadah (the woman with irregular bleeding) to identify which bleeding is hayd (menstruation) and which is istihada (pathological bleeding), applying the principles of discernment (tamyiz), habituation (ta'yid), and return to the average ('adah). His sensitivity to the practical difficulties these rulings present for women with irregular cycles is evident throughout.
